item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I didn't even realize they'd been moved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For anyone else who had trouble seeing the multiple endings and found that PeppersGhosts's links were broken:
Ending A
Ending B
Ending C

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also vote Dr. Ricks

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love everything about this except the endings. Even with the multiple-ending touch, any kind of resolution to this at all is a letdown to me, particularly since they undercut the subtle horror you have going here. Anyway, I seem to be in the minority on that, and the imagery here is just fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this! I got a chill during Dalton's interview - "Sometimes when you press your ear to them, and the music goes quiet between songs, you can hear them breathing and groaning. Please, Dr. Ricks. You're the only true friend I have in the world. Please don't stop talking to me…" - this was paced just right, a little reminder of the horrible fate in store for these people before the last event log.

(By any chance, is the photo used for this SCP from the carousel at House on the Rock? I love that place; it's a total gold mine for anomalous/cursed objects. +1 from this Wisconsin resident.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had no idea there was a Clue-inspired SCP! This is great. It suffers from a few of the same problems as Clue (Endings A and B are both kind of dull, especially when compared to Ending C which is so much better) but all three being linked to in the comments section helps to nullify that a little.

I also get Circle vibes from it (a movie where a bunch of people stand in a circle and vote who to kill), which is also good. The characters here feel real, and you get a good idea of who they are/their personality from these short interviews. This really does feel like a classic little mystery, I even went back to look for clues after I got my ending (A).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it! but one thing, the ending with doctor Rick should have the containment procedures revised, there aren't people on the carousel after that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ending C is my headcanon. There's something so… delightful about it, in a twisted way. But all three are well written, the item itself is interesting, and I enjoyed the whole thing beginning to end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this. What a demented concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that the contest is over, I've gone ahead and prettied up the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. This was a fun read. You really do a bang up job with the characterization, and all the endings are all top notch.

+1 PG. This was a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you liked it!

That's actually the sort of mental image I was going for! I chose the Rockies to hammer home the idea that this thing really doesn't belong there. The incongruity of a functioning carousel out in the middle of the far-off wilderness appeals to me on a deep level. I didn't want to set it in a forest, though—a certain someone already did a skip about a spooky merry-go-round in the woods. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got Ending A first, and I choose it to be my canon. Love this. Holy fucking shit. A++

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I upvoted this pretty early on; I think it's very strong, and — while the three ending trick feels a little shoe-horned, the endings themselves are strong enough for them to work for me.

I've got one suggestion — it's tentative, and not super-important, but since I'm going through these pieces I thought I'd drop it here:

FEEDBACK
– hide block

SCP-3306 is a carousel amusement ride located in the Continental Ranges of the Canadian Rockies. It is powered by anomalous means and remains in constant motion.

When I first read this, the immediate image this opening description gave me was of a carousel sitting on the side of a frozen mountain.

I think this might benefit from putting the carousel somewhere where it's less jarring (but still significantly creepy). A derelict amusement park might be too cliché; an alternative might be an abandoned town or settlement — or even a warehouse or storage basement (I particularly like this solution, as it's easy to imagine a carousel locked away in a warehouse or underground, and it also prevents the people on the carousel from being affected by inclement weather).

Anyway, I really enjoyed this one — good luck with the contest!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Really, really impressed by the style of mystery presented here. I regret not reading this sooner, it's got to be my favorite Murder-Mystery submission thus far.

More people need to come check this one out.

ETA: I ended my first read with ending C, which is the best in my opinion - I don't think I would have been quite as impressed by ending A as much, even moreso for B.

Post-contest, I'd like to see a little bit more added to the interviews. I feel like I would have appreciated a bit more back-and forth on the victims' speculations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Realize that I started this comment, had trouble posting, and came back an hour later to post again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Three endings. I've added some links to my author post in case others have the same issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How many endings are there? I've only managed to get two after many refreshes, and it seems like it sticks with the same one for a long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the risk of making a terrible pun, this was a heck of a ride.

Also what do you mean there are multiple endings now I have to read more goddammit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the lovely comment! Glad you liked it.

As for the format screw, I did consider giving a stronger implication that there's an in-universe justification for it. The multiple timeline weirdness is an aspect of the anomaly, but not the main focus of it, and thus it got sidelined so as not to detract from the mystery at hand. Perhaps with more time I could have found an elegant way to include that plot thread, but, alas. I do think the format screw fits nicely into the underlying theme of the anomaly, though (i.e. this is what happens when trickster gods play parlor games).

Apparently in my mad dash to finish this, I straight-up forgot what Jeannie's surname was when I went to write the endings. Nice catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. Of all the entries, this was the one that got me most invested in the actual mystery-solving aspect; it hits the right spot between being simple and easy to follow, whilst retaining enough room for thought and nuance and lacking an obvious solution. The characterisation is also well done - it's concise while being detailed enough to invest the reader sufficiently in the characters and remember who is who.

Although I liked the multiple endings as well, and felt they added a lot of rereading mileage to the article, my only gripe would be that their inclusion felt a little forced; I would have accepted them more easily in a piece about an SCP that affected documentation or created parallel dimensions, for example, while there doesn't seem to be any reason for them to be in an article about this object other than wanting to include them for out-of-universe reasons.

PoI-28-Jeannie seems to switch between Kirkwood and Kirkland, which I think is an error unless there's something I'm missing.

Anyway, if this were to win I think it would be a solid choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SIX INTERVIEWS AND A FORMAT SCREW Y'ALL.

I had to write for, like, eight hours straight, but I got everything done that I wanted to get done. I'll probably touch this up further after the contest is over.

In case the header tipped you off: yes, this one changes. The Clue movie is one of my favorite films, and it inspired me to do a whodunnit-style mystery with multiple endings.

Be warned: this is the coldest of posts.

+ Links to ending pages
– hide block

This is how it could have happened.
But how about this?
Although this is how it probably went down.

Discover more disconcerting carnival rides in
~PeppersGhost's Gallery of Illusion!~
